planting.note: Plant divisions or rooted cuttings in spring in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; mother of thyme forms a tighter, denser mat than T. serpyllum and is the preferred species for most lawn-substitute and stepping stone plantings.

directions: Plant divisions or rooted cuttings in spring in well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; excellent drainage is essential — plants will rot in waterlogged soil. Thymus praecox is a low, mat-forming thyme of central and southern European rocky terrain, producing very flat carpets of tiny, dark-green, aromatic leaves 1–4 inches tall that bear a profusion of bright magenta-pink to lilac flowers in early summer smothered with honeybees; it forms a tighter, denser mat than wild thyme (T. serpyllum) and is the preferred species for most ground-cover, stepping-stone, and lawn-substitute plantings. The aromatic leaves are fully culinary-quality; the plant tolerates foot traffic and shearing well. Subsp. arcticus (with cultivars 'Coccineus', 'Purple Creeping') offers deep red-purple flowers for ornamental use.

planting.note: Plant divisions or rooted cuttings in spring in moist, rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; contain in pots or with underground root barriers as peppermint spreads aggressively by stolons.

directions: Plant divisions or rooted cuttings in spring in moist, rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; contain in buried pots or with root barriers sunk 12 inches into the soil as peppermint spreads aggressively by underground stolons and will colonize a large area if uncontained. Mentha × piperita is a sterile hybrid between water mint (M. aquatica) and spearmint (M. spicata) that is propagated vegetatively; it is the mint whose essential oil (menthol) is used in toothpaste, confections, medicine, cosmetics, and a vast range of products, and whose leaves are used for peppermint tea, juleps, mojitos, and as a culinary flavoring. Black peppermint (var. piperita) has purple-tinged stems and the strongest menthol flavor; white peppermint (var. officinalis) has greener stems and is slightly milder. Both types prefer moist, fertile soil and appreciate afternoon shade in hot climates.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; contain with root barriers as spearmint is vigorously stoloniferous — container planting is the most reliable method for small gardens.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; spearmint tolerates light shade better than most culinary herbs and is one of the better mints for partially shaded kitchen gardens. Mentha spicata is the garden mint of European tradition with smooth, bright-green, wrinkled leaves and a sweet, relatively cool, characteristic mint flavor (from carvone rather than menthol) used in mint sauce with lamb, jelly, juleps, mojitos, tabbouleh, and Middle Eastern cuisine. Unlike peppermint (M. × piperita) which is a sterile hybrid, spearmint sets viable seed and can be started from seed (though vegetative propagation produces more reliable, uniform plants). It is generally considered a milder, sweeter mint than peppermint and is the preferred choice in savory cooking and cocktails; cultivars 'Kentucky Colonel' and 'Moroccan' are popular selections with especially fine flavor.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; apple mint is more drought-tolerant and more vigorous than most mints and adapts to a wider range of soil conditions.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; apple mint is more drought-tolerant and adaptable than peppermint or spearmint and tolerates poorer, drier soils. Mentha suaveolens is a densely woolly-leaved European mint producing large, round, velvety, gray-green leaves with a pleasant, fruity mint flavor with mild apple overtones; it is especially valued for making mint jelly, apple-mint sorbet, fruit salads, and flavored vinegars, and is an attractive, textural contrast to smooth-leaved herbs in the garden. The variegated cultivar 'Variegata' (pineapple mint) has cream-edged leaves and is one of the most ornamental of all mints, excellent in containers. Apple mint is more heat-tolerant than most mints and performs well in the Gulf South if given afternoon shade and consistent moisture.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade near streams and water; horse mint is the wild mint most adapted to moist, streamside conditions.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; horse mint thrives in consistently moist to wet conditions along stream banks, pond margins, and in rain gardens, tolerating more wet soil than most mints. Mentha longifolia is a tall, robust, wild mint native from Europe to western Asia with long, narrow, silvery-hairy, gray-green leaves and tall spikes of pale lilac to white flowers in mid- to late summer that are excellent for pollinator plantings; the flavor and aroma are strongly minty but somewhat coarser than spearmint or peppermint. It is used as a culinary herb in Middle Eastern cooking, especially in Lebanon and Syria where it is the traditional mint for tea, and in South Africa where it is called "kruisement." The silvery foliage is unusually ornamental among mints and provides textural contrast in herb borders.

planting.note: Sow direct 4–6 weeks before last frost in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; purple giant hyssop is one of the largest native agastaches, growing to 4–6 feet tall in moist, fertile soils.

directions: Sow direct 4–6 weeks before last frost in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; purple giant hyssop tolerates partial shade better than most agastaches and adapts to the moist, fertile soils of woodland edges and naturalized meadow gardens. Agastache scrophulariifolia is an impressive, tall (4–6 feet) native perennial of eastern North American open woodlands and forest edges producing large, mint-scented, heart-shaped leaves and tall, dense spikes of violet-purple flowers in mid- to late summer that are outstanding for native bees (especially bumblebees), butterflies, and hummingbirds. The plant is one of the most prolific native pollinator plants available for the North American garden; it self-seeds moderately to naturalize in favorable spots and is excellent at the back of native plant borders, along fence lines, and in naturalized meadow plantings. The leaves and flowers are edible and were used medicinally by several Native American peoples.
